question:
For a set  $S$  of nonnegative integers, let  $r_S(n)$  denote the number of
ordered pairs  $(s_1, s_2)$  such that  $s_1 \in S$, $s_2 \in S$, $s_1 \ne
s_2$,
and  $s_1 + s_2 = n$.  Is it possible to partition the nonnegative
integers into two sets  $A$  and  $B$  in such a way that  $r_A(n) = r_B(n)$
for all  $n$ ?
solution:
\textbf{First solution:} Yes, such a partition is possible. To achieve it,
place each integer into $A$ if it has an even number of 1s in its binary
representation, and into $B$ if it has an odd number. (One discovers this
by simply attempting to place the first few numbers by hand and noticing
the resulting pattern.)

To show that $r_A(n) = r_B(n)$, we exhibit a bijection between the pairs
$(a_1, a_2)$ of distinct elements of $A$ with $a_1 + a_2 = n$ and the
pairs $(b_1, b_2)$ of distinct elements of $B$ with $b_1 + b_2 = n$.
Namely, given a pair $(a_1, a_2)$ with $a_1+a_2 = n$, write both numbers
in binary and find the lowest-order place in which they differ (such a
place exists because $a_1 \neq a_2$). Change both numbers in that place
and call the resulting numbers $b_1, b_2$. Then $a_1 + a_2 = b_1 + b_2 =
n$, but the parity of the number of 1s in $b_1$ is opposite that of $a_1$,
and likewise between $b_2$ and $a_2$. This yields the desired bijection.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Micah Smukler)
Write $b(n)$ for the number of 1s in the base 2 expansion of $n$,
and $f(n) = (-1)^{b(n)}$.
Then
the desired partition can be described as $A = f^{-1}(1)$ and $B = f^{-1}(-1)$.
Since $f(2n) + f(2n+1) = 0$, we have
\[
\sum_{i=0}^n f(n) = \begin{cases} 0 & \mbox{$n$ odd} \\
f(n) & \mbox{$n$ even.} \end{cases}
\]
If $p,q$ are both in $A$, then $f(p) + f(q) = 2$;
if $p,q$ are both in $B$, then $f(p) + f(q) = -2$; if $p,q$ are
in different sets, then $f(p) + f(q) = 0$. In other words,
\[
2(r_A(n) - r_B(n)) = \sum_{p+q=n,p < q} (f(p) + f(q))
\]
and it suffices to show that the sum on the right is always zero.
If $n$ is odd, that sum is visibly $\sum_{i=0}^n f(i) = 0$.
If $n$ is even, the sum equals
\[
\left(\sum_{i=0}^n f(i) \right) - f(n/2) = f(n) - f(n/2) = 0.
\]
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Put $f(x) = \sum_{n \in A} x^n$ and $g(x) = \sum_{n \in B} x^n$; then
the value of $r_A(n)$ (resp.\ $r_B(n)$) is the coefficient of $x^n$
in $f(x)^2 - f(x^2)$ (resp.\ $g(x)^2 - g(x^2)$). From the evident identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{1-x} &= f(x) + g(x) \\
f(x) &= f(x^2) + xg(x^2) \\
g(x) &= g(x^2) + xf(x^2),
\end{align*}
we have
\begin{align*}
f(x) - g(x) &= f(x^2) - g(x^2) + xg(x^2) - xf(x^2) \\
&= (1-x)(f(x^2) - g(x^2)) \\
&= \frac{f(x^2) - g(x^2)}{f(x) + g(x)}.
\end{align*}
We deduce that $f(x)^2 - g(x)^2 = f(x^2) - g(x^2)$, yielding the desired
equality.

\textbf{Note:}
This partition is actually unique, up to interchanging $A$ and $B$.
More precisely, the condition that $0 \in A$ and $r_A(n) = r_B(n)$ for
$n=1, \dots, m$ uniquely determines the positions of $0, \dots, m$. We
see this by induction on $m$: given the result for $m-1$, switching the
location of $m$ changes $r_A(m)$ by one and does not change $r_B(m)$, so
it is not possible for both positions to work.  Robin Chapman points out
this problem is solved in D.J. Newman's \textit{Analytic Number Theory}
(Springer, 1998); in that solution, one uses generating functions to find
the partition and establish its uniqueness, not just verify it.

descriptive_long.question:
For a set  $integerset$  of nonnegative integers, let  $paircountset(targetsum)$  denote the number of
ordered pairs  $(firstmember, secondmember)$  such that  $firstmember \in integerset$, $secondmember \in integerset$, $firstmember \ne
secondmember$,
and  $firstmember + secondmember = targetsum$.  Is it possible to partition the nonnegative
integers into two sets  $alfaset$  and  $bravoset$  in such a way that  $paircountalfa(targetsum) = paircountbravo(targetsum)$
for all  $targetsum$ ?
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} Yes, such a partition is possible. To achieve it,
place each integer into $alfaset$ if it has an even number of 1s in its binary
representation, and into $bravoset$ if it has an odd number. (One discovers this
by simply attempting to place the first few numbers by hand and noticing
the resulting pattern.)

To show that $paircountalfa(targetsum) = paircountbravo(targetsum)$, we exhibit a bijection between the pairs
$(a_1, a_2)$ of distinct elements of $alfaset$ with $a_1 + a_2 = targetsum$ and the
pairs $(b_1, b_2)$ of distinct elements of $bravoset$ with $b_1 + b_2 = targetsum$.
Namely, given a pair $(a_1, a_2)$ with $a_1+a_2 = targetsum$, write both numbers
in binary and find the lowest-order place in which they differ (such a
place exists because $a_1 \neq a_2$). Change both numbers in that place
and call the resulting numbers $b_1, b_2$. Then $a_1 + a_2 = b_1 + b_2 =
targetsum$, but the parity of the number of 1s in $b_1$ is opposite that of $a_1$,
and likewise between $b_2$ and $a_2$. This yields the desired bijection.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Micah Smukler)
Write $bitcount(targetsum)$ for the number of 1s in the base 2 expansion of $targetsum$,
and $parityfunc(targetsum) = (-1)^{bitcount(targetsum)}$.
Then
the desired partition can be described as $alfaset = parityfunc^{-1}(1)$ and $bravoset = parityfunc^{-1}(-1)$.
Since $parityfunc(2targetsum) + parityfunc(2targetsum+1) = 0$, we have
\[
\sum_{loopindex=0}^{targetsum} parityfunc(targetsum) = \begin{cases} 0 & \mbox{$targetsum$ odd} \\
parityfunc(targetsum) & \mbox{$targetsum$ even.} \end{cases}
\]
If $firstindex,secondindex$ are both in $alfaset$, then $parityfunc(firstindex) + parityfunc(secondindex) = 2$;
if $firstindex,secondindex$ are both in $bravoset$, then $parityfunc(firstindex) + parityfunc(secondindex) = -2$; if $firstindex,secondindex$ are
in different sets, then $parityfunc(firstindex) + parityfunc(secondindex) = 0$. In other words,
\[
2(paircountalfa(targetsum) - paircountbravo(targetsum)) = \sum_{firstindex+secondindex=targetsum,firstindex < secondindex} (parityfunc(firstindex) + parityfunc(secondindex))
\]
and it suffices to show that the sum on the right is always zero.
If $targetsum$ is odd, that sum is visibly $\sum_{loopindex=0}^{targetsum} parityfunc(loopindex) = 0$.
If $targetsum$ is even, the sum equals
\[
\left(\sum_{loopindex=0}^{targetsum} parityfunc(loopindex) \right) - parityfunc(targetsum/2) = parityfunc(targetsum) - parityfunc(targetsum/2) = 0.
\]
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Put $parityfunc(formalvar) = \sum_{targetsum \in alfaset} formalvar^{targetsum}$ and $complementfunc(formalvar) = \sum_{targetsum \in bravoset} formalvar^{targetsum}$; then
the value of $paircountalfa(targetsum)$ (resp.\ $paircountbravo(targetsum)$) is the coefficient of $formalvar^{targetsum}$
in $parityfunc(formalvar)^2 - parityfunc(formalvar^2)$ (resp.\ $complementfunc(formalvar)^2 - complementfunc(formalvar^2)$). From the evident identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{1-formalvar} &= parityfunc(formalvar) + complementfunc(formalvar) \\
parityfunc(formalvar) &= parityfunc(formalvar^2) + formalvar\,complementfunc(formalvar^2) \\
complementfunc(formalvar) &= complementfunc(formalvar^2) + formalvar\,parityfunc(formalvar^2),
\end{align*}
we have
\begin{align*}
parityfunc(formalvar) - complementfunc(formalvar) &= parityfunc(formalvar^2) - complementfunc(formalvar^2) + formalvar\,complementfunc(formalvar^2) - formalvar\,parityfunc(formalvar^2) \\
&= (1-formalvar)(parityfunc(formalvar^2) - complementfunc(formalvar^2)) \\
&= \frac{parityfunc(formalvar^2) - complementfunc(formalvar^2)}{parityfunc(formalvar) + complementfunc(formalvar)}.
\end{align*}
We deduce that $parityfunc(formalvar)^2 - complementfunc(formalvar)^2 = parityfunc(formalvar^2) - complementfunc(formalvar^2)$, yielding the desired
equality.

\textbf{Note:}
This partition is actually unique, up to interchanging $alfaset$ and $bravoset$.
More precisely, the condition that $0 \in alfaset$ and $paircountalfa(targetsum) = paircountbravo(targetsum)$ for
$targetsum=1, \dots, indexlimit$ uniquely determines the positions of $0, \dots, indexlimit$. We
see this by induction on $indexlimit$: given the result for $indexlimit-1$, switching the
location of $indexlimit$ changes $paircountalfa(indexlimit)$ by one and does not change $paircountbravo(indexlimit)$, so
it is not possible for both positions to work.  Robin Chapman points out
this problem is solved in D.J. Newman's \textit{Analytic Number Theory}
(Springer, 1998); in that solution, one uses generating functions to find
the partition and establish its uniqueness, not just verify it.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For a set  $waterfall$  of nonnegative integers, let  $wilderness(blueberries)$  denote the number of
ordered pairs  $(lanterns, fireworks)$  such that  $lanterns \in waterfall$, $fireworks \in waterfall$, $lanterns \ne
fireworks$,
and  $lanterns + fireworks = blueberries$.  Is it possible to partition the nonnegative
integers into two sets  $galaxycar$  and  $drizzleof$  in such a way that  $stormcaves(blueberries) = cobblestone(blueberries)$
for all  $blueberries$ ?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} Yes, such a partition is possible. To achieve it,
place each integer into $galaxycar$ if it has an even number of 1s in its binary
representation, and into $drizzleof$ if it has an odd number. (One discovers this
by simply attempting to place the first few numbers by hand and noticing
the resulting pattern.)

To show that $stormcaves(blueberries) = cobblestone(blueberries)$, we exhibit a bijection between the pairs
$(a_1, a_2)$ of distinct elements of $galaxycar$ with $a_1 + a_2 = blueberries$ and the
pairs $(b_1, b_2)$ of distinct elements of $drizzleof$ with $b_1 + b_2 = blueberries$.
Namely, given a pair $(a_1, a_2)$ with $a_1+a_2 = blueberries$, write both numbers
in binary and find the lowest-order place in which they differ (such a
place exists because $a_1 \neq a_2$). Change both numbers in that place
and call the resulting numbers $b_1, b_2$. Then $a_1 + a_2 = b_1 + b_2 =
blueberries$, but the parity of the number of 1s in $b_1$ is opposite that of $a_1$,
and likewise between $b_2$ and $a_2$. This yields the desired bijection.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Micah Smukler)
Write $marigolds(blueberries)$ for the number of 1s in the base 2 expansion of $blueberries$,
and $heirlooms(blueberries) = (-1)^{marigolds(blueberries)}$.
Then
the desired partition can be described as $galaxycar = heirlooms^{-1}(1)$ and $drizzleof = heirlooms^{-1}(-1)$.
Since $heirlooms(2blueberries) + heirlooms(2blueberries+1) = 0$, we have
\[
\sum_{needlepin=0}^{blueberries} heirlooms(blueberries) = \begin{cases} 0 & \mbox{$blueberries$ odd} \\
heirlooms(blueberries) & \mbox{$blueberries$ even.} \end{cases}
\]
If $rainspout,sledgesnow$ are both in $galaxycar$, then $heirlooms(rainspout) + heirlooms(sledgesnow) = 2$;
if $rainspout,sledgesnow$ are both in $drizzleof$, then $heirlooms(rainspout) + heirlooms(sledgesnow) = -2$; if $rainspout,sledgesnow$ are
in different sets, then $heirlooms(rainspout) + heirlooms(sledgesnow) = 0$. In other words,
\[
2(stormcaves(blueberries) - cobblestone(blueberries)) = \sum_{rainspout+sledgesnow=blueberries,rainspout < sledgesnow} (heirlooms(rainspout) + heirlooms(sledgesnow))
\]
and it suffices to show that the sum on the right is always zero.
If $blueberries$ is odd, that sum is visibly $\sum_{needlepin=0}^{blueberries} heirlooms(needlepin) = 0$.
If $blueberries$ is even, the sum equals
\[
\left(\sum_{needlepin=0}^{blueberries} heirlooms(needlepin) \right) - heirlooms(blueberries/2) = heirlooms(blueberries) - heirlooms(blueberries/2) = 0.
\]
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Put $heirlooms(tangerine) = \sum_{blueberries \in galaxycar} tangerine^{blueberries}$ and $sunscreen(tangerine) = \sum_{blueberries \in drizzleof} tangerine^{blueberries}$; then
the value of $stormcaves(blueberries)$ (resp.\ $cobblestone(blueberries)$) is the coefficient of $tangerine^{blueberries}$
in $heirlooms(tangerine)^2 - heirlooms(tangerine^2)$ (resp.\ $sunscreen(tangerine)^2 - sunscreen(tangerine^2)$). From the evident identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{1-tangerine} &= heirlooms(tangerine) + sunscreen(tangerine) \\
heirlooms(tangerine) &= heirlooms(tangerine^2) + tangerine\,sunscreen(tangerine^2) \\
sunscreen(tangerine) &= sunscreen(tangerine^2) + tangerine\,heirlooms(tangerine^2),
\end{align*}
we have
\begin{align*}
heirlooms(tangerine) - sunscreen(tangerine) &= heirlooms(tangerine^2) - sunscreen(tangerine^2) + tangerine\,sunscreen(tangerine^2) - tangerine\,heirlooms(tangerine^2) \\
&= (1-tangerine)(heirlooms(tangerine^2) - sunscreen(tangerine^2)) \\
&= \frac{heirlooms(tangerine^2) - sunscreen(tangerine^2)}{heirlooms(tangerine) + sunscreen(tangerine)}.
\end{align*}
We deduce that $heirlooms(tangerine)^2 - sunscreen(tangerine)^2 = heirlooms(tangerine^2) - sunscreen(tangerine^2)$, yielding the desired
equality.

\textbf{Note:}
This partition is actually unique, up to interchanging $galaxycar$ and $drizzleof$.
More precisely, the condition that $0 \in galaxycar$ and $stormcaves(blueberries) = cobblestone(blueberries)$ for
$blueberries=1, \dots, patchwork$ uniquely determines the positions of $0, \dots, patchwork$. We
see this by induction on $patchwork$: given the result for $patchwork-1$, switching the
location of $patchwork$ changes $stormcaves(patchwork)$ by one and does not change $cobblestone(patchwork)$, so
it is not possible for both positions to work.  Robin Chapman points out
this problem is solved in D.J. Newman's \textit{Analytic Number Theory}
(Springer, 1998); in that solution, one uses generating functions to find
the partition and establish its uniqueness, not just verify it.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For a set  $orderedlist$  of nonnegative integers, let  $uniqueness(continuum)$  denote the number of
ordered pairs  $(aggregate, totality)$  such that  $aggregate \in orderedlist$, $totality \in orderedlist$, $aggregate \ne
totality$, and  $aggregate + totality = continuum$.  Is it possible to partition the nonnegative
integers into two sets  $complement$  and  $superset$  in such a way that  $emptiness(continuum) = voidness(continuum)$
for all  $continuum$ ?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} Yes, such a partition is possible. To achieve it,
place each integer into $complement$ if it has an even number of 1s in its binary
representation, and into $superset$ if it has an odd number. (One discovers this
by simply attempting to place the first few numbers by hand and noticing
the resulting pattern.)

To show that $emptiness(continuum) = voidness(continuum)$, we exhibit a bijection between the pairs
$(a_1, a_2)$ of distinct elements of $complement$ with $a_1 + a_2 = continuum$ and the
pairs $(b_1, b_2)$ of distinct elements of $superset$ with $b_1 + b_2 = continuum$.
Namely, given a pair $(a_1, a_2)$ with $a_1+a_2 = continuum$, write both numbers
in binary and find the lowest-order place in which they differ (such a
place exists because $a_1 \neq a_2$). Change both numbers in that place
and call the resulting numbers $b_1, b_2$. Then $a_1 + a_2 = b_1 + b_2 =
continuum$, but the parity of the number of 1s in $b_1$ is opposite that of $a_1$,
and likewise between $b_2$ and $a_2$. This yields the desired bijection.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Micah Smukler)
Write $nullness(continuum)$ for the number of 1s in the base 2 expansion of $continuum$,
and $constancy(continuum) = (-1)^{nullness(continuum)}$.
Then
the desired partition can be described as $complement = constancy^{-1}(1)$ and $superset = constancy^{-1}(-1)$.
Since $constancy(2continuum) + constancy(2continuum+1) = 0$, we have
\[
\sum_{terminal=0}^{continuum} constancy(continuum) = \begin{cases} 0 & \mbox{$continuum$ odd} \\
constancy(continuum) & \mbox{$continuum$ even.} \end{cases}
\]
If $holistic,entirety$ are both in $complement$, then $constancy(holistic) + constancy(entirety) = 2$;
if $holistic,entirety$ are both in $superset$, then $constancy(holistic) + constancy(entirety) = -2$; if $holistic,entirety$ are
in different sets, then $constancy(holistic) + constancy(entirety) = 0$. In other words,
\[
2(emptiness(continuum) - voidness(continuum)) = \sum_{holistic+entirety=continuum,holistic < entirety} (constancy(holistic) + constancy(entirety))
\]
and it suffices to show that the sum on the right is always zero.
If $continuum$ is odd, that sum is visibly $\sum_{terminal=0}^{continuum} constancy(terminal) = 0$.
If $continuum$ is even, the sum equals
\[
\left(\sum_{terminal=0}^{continuum} constancy(terminal) \right) - constancy(continuum/2) = constancy(continuum) - constancy(continuum/2) = 0.
\]
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Put $constancy(constant) = \sum_{continuum \in complement} constant^{continuum}$ and $destroyer(constant) = \sum_{continuum \in superset} constant^{continuum}$; then
the value of $emptiness(continuum)$ (resp.\ $voidness(continuum)$) is the coefficient of $constant^{continuum}$
in $constancy(constant)^2 - constancy(constant^2)$ (resp.\ $destroyer(constant)^2 - destroyer(constant^2)$). From the evident identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{1-constant} &= constancy(constant) + destroyer(constant) \\
constancy(constant) &= constancy(constant^2) + constant\,destroyer(constant^2) \\
destroyer(constant) &= destroyer(constant^2) + constant\,constancy(constant^2),
\end{align*}
we have
\begin{align*}
constancy(constant) - destroyer(constant) &= constancy(constant^2) - destroyer(constant^2) + constant\,destroyer(constant^2) - constant\,constancy(constant^2) \\
&= (1-constant)(constancy(constant^2) - destroyer(constant^2)) \\
&= \frac{constancy(constant^2) - destroyer(constant^2)}{constancy(constant) + destroyer(constant)}.
\end{align*}
We deduce that $constancy(constant)^2 - destroyer(constant)^2 = constancy(constant^2) - destroyer(constant^2)$, yielding the desired
equality.

\textbf{Note:}
This partition is actually unique, up to interchanging $complement$ and $superset$.
More precisely, the condition that $0 \in complement$ and $emptiness(continuum) = voidness(continuum)$ for
$continuum=1, \dots, nothingness$ uniquely determines the positions of $0, \dots, nothingness$. We
see this by induction on $nothingness$: given the result for $nothingness-1$, switching the
location of $nothingness$ changes $emptiness(nothingness)$ by one and does not change $voidness(nothingness)$, so
it is not possible for both positions to work.  Robin Chapman points out
this problem is solved in D.J. Newman's \textit{Analytic Number Theory}
(Springer, 1998); in that solution, one uses generating functions to find
the partition and establish its uniqueness, not just verify it.

garbled_string.question: For a set  $qzxwvtnp$  of nonnegative integers, let  $wemtgqrs(hjgrksla)$  denote the number of ordered pairs  $(mvbtrqsp, plkzmnob)$  such that  $mvbtrqsp \in qzxwvtnp$, $plkzmnob \in qzxwvtnp$, $mvbtrqsp \ne plkzmnob$, and  $mvbtrqsp + plkzmnob = hjgrksla$.  Is it possible to partition the nonnegative integers into two sets  $gldrfqwe$  and  $snvkjdap$  in such a way that  $yokspndl(hjgrksla) = cfrqpmza(hjgrksla)$ for all  $hjgrksla$ ?
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} Yes, such a partition is possible. To achieve it,
place each integer into $gldrfqwe$ if it has an even number of 1s in its binary
representation, and into $snvkjdap$ if it has an odd number. (One discovers this
by simply attempting to place the first few numbers by hand and noticing
the resulting pattern.)

To show that $yokspndl(hjgrksla) = cfrqpmza(hjgrksla)$, we exhibit a bijection between the pairs
$(a_1, a_2)$ of distinct elements of $gldrfqwe$ with $a_1 + a_2 = hjgrksla$ and the
pairs $(b_1, b_2)$ of distinct elements of $snvkjdap$ with $b_1 + b_2 = hjgrksla$.
Namely, given a pair $(a_1, a_2)$ with $a_1+a_2 = hjgrksla$, write both numbers
in binary and find the lowest-order place in which they differ (such a
place exists because $a_1 \neq a_2$). Change both numbers in that place
and call the resulting numbers $b_1, b_2$. Then $a_1 + a_2 = b_1 + b_2 =
hjgrksla$, but the parity of the number of 1s in $b_1$ is opposite that of $a_1$,
and likewise between $b_2$ and $a_2$. This yields the desired bijection.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Micah Smukler)
Write $djlwqopa(hjgrksla)$ for the number of 1s in the base 2 expansion of $hjgrksla$,
and $rixhumtn(hjgrksla) = (-1)^{djlwqopa(hjgrksla)}$.
Then
the desired partition can be described as $gldrfqwe = rixhumtn^{-1}(1)$ and $snvkjdap = rixhumtn^{-1}(-1)$.
Since $rixhumtn(2hjgrksla) + rixhumtn(2hjgrksla+1) = 0$, we have
\[
\sum_{ebaxchud=0}^{hjgrksla} rixhumtn(hjgrksla) = \begin{cases} 0 & \mbox{$hjgrksla$ odd} \\
rixhumtn(hjgrksla) & \mbox{$hjgrksla$ even.} \end{cases}
\]
If $zoirvmac,tclsopkw$ are both in $gldrfqwe$, then $rixhumtn(zoirvmac) + rixhumtn(tclsopkw) = 2$;
if $zoirvmac,tclsopkw$ are both in $snvkjdap$, then $rixhumtn(zoirvmac) + rixhumtn(tclsopkw) = -2$; if $zoirvmac,tclsopkw$ are
in different sets, then $rixhumtn(zoirvmac) + rixhumtn(tclsopkw) = 0$. In other words,
\[
2(yokspndl(hjgrksla) - cfrqpmza(hjgrksla)) = \sum_{zoirvmac+tclsopkw=hjgrksla,zoirvmac < tclsopkw} (rixhumtn(zoirvmac) + rixhumtn(tclsopkw))
\]
and it suffices to show that the sum on the right is always zero.
If $hjgrksla$ is odd, that sum is visibly $\sum_{ebaxchud=0}^{hjgrksla} rixhumtn(ebaxchud) = 0$.
If $hjgrksla$ is even, the sum equals
\[
\left(\sum_{ebaxchud=0}^{hjgrksla} rixhumtn(ebaxchud) \right) - rixhumtn(hjgrksla/2) = rixhumtn(hjgrksla) - rixhumtn(hjgrksla/2) = 0.
\]
This yields the desired result.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Put $rixhumtn(nzymplka) = \sum_{hjgrksla \in gldrfqwe} nzymplka^{hjgrksla}$ and $fvqzdnpm(nzymplka) = \sum_{hjgrksla \in snvkjdap} nzymplka^{hjgrksla}$; then
the value of $yokspndl(hjgrksla)$ (resp.\ $cfrqpmza(hjgrksla)$) is the coefficient of $nzymplka^{hjgrksla}$
in $rixhumtn(nzymplka)^2 - rixhumtn(nzymplka^2)$ (resp.\ $fvqzdnpm(nzymplka)^2 - fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2)$). From the evident identities
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{1-nzymplka} &= rixhumtn(nzymplka) + fvqzdnpm(nzymplka) \\
rixhumtn(nzymplka) &= rixhumtn(nzymplka^2) + nzymplka fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2) \\
fvqzdnpm(nzymplka) &= fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2) + nzymplka rixhumtn(nzymplka^2),
\end{align*}
we have
\begin{align*}
rixhumtn(nzymplka) - fvqzdnpm(nzymplka) &= rixhumtn(nzymplka^2) - fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2) + nzymplka fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2) - nzymplka rixhumtn(nzymplka^2) \\
&= (1-nzymplka)(rixhumtn(nzymplka^2) - fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2)) \\
&= \frac{rixhumtn(nzymplka^2) - fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2)}{rixhumtn(nzymplka) + fvqzdnpm(nzymplka)}.
\end{align*}
We deduce that $rixhumtn(nzymplka)^2 - fvqzdnpm(nzymplka)^2 = rixhumtn(nzymplka^2) - fvqzdnpm(nzymplka^2)$, yielding the desired
equality.

\textbf{Note:}
This partition is actually unique, up to interchanging $gldrfqwe$ and $snvkjdap$.
More precisely, the condition that $0 \in gldrfqwe$ and $yokspndl(hjgrksla) = cfrqpmza(hjgrksla)$ for
$hjgrksla=1, \dots, hkarwbce$ uniquely determines the positions of $0, \dots, hkarwbce$. We
see this by induction on $hkarwbce$: given the result for $hkarwbce-1$, switching the
location of $hkarwbce$ changes $yokspndl(hkarwbce)$ by one and does not change $cfrqpmza(hkarwbce)$, so
it is not possible for both positions to work.  Robin Chapman points out
this problem is solved in D.J. Newman's \textit{Analytic Number Theory}
(Springer, 1998); in that solution, one uses generating functions to find
the partition and establish its uniqueness, not just verify it.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $d\ge 1$ and write every point  
$\mathbf x=(x_1,\dots ,x_d)\in\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$ in base 2,
     $x_k=\sum_{j\ge 0}2^j b_{j,k}$ with $b_{j,k}\in\{0,1\}$.
Put  

  $\displaystyle\omega(\mathbf x)=\sum_{j\ge 0}\sum_{k=1}^{d} b_{j,k}\pmod 2$  

(the total number of binary 1's in all coordinates, taken modulo 2) and define  

  $A=\{\mathbf x: \omega(\mathbf x)=0\},\qquad  
  B=\{\mathbf x: \omega(\mathbf x)=1\}. $  

For a set $S\subset\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$ let  

  $\displaystyle u_S(\mathbf n)=\#\bigl\{\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}\subset S:
        \mathbf s_1\ne\mathbf s_2,\;
        \mathbf s_1+\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n\bigr\}$  
(the sum is component-wise).

(a)  Prove that $u_A(\mathbf n)=u_B(\mathbf n)$ for every
    $\mathbf n\in\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$.  
    Supply two independent proofs:
        * a purely combinatorial fixed-point-free involution, and  
        * an argument with multivariate generating functions.

(b)  Show that, once the colour of the origin is prescribed, the
    partition $\mathbb N_0^{\,d}=A\sqcup B$ is unique up to swapping the
    two parts.
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  For $\ell\ge 0$ set  
$e_{k,\ell}=2^{\ell}\mathbf e_k$ where $\mathbf e_k$ is the $k$-th unit
vector in $\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$; write $\mathbf b_\ell(\mathbf x)=(b_{\,\ell,1},\dots ,b_{\,\ell,d})$ for the $\ell$-th
binary column of $\mathbf x$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A.  Combinatorial bijection
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Input: an unordered pair
$\{\mathbf a,\mathbf c\}\subset A$ with $\mathbf a\!+\!\mathbf c=\mathbf n$
and $\mathbf a\neq\mathbf c$.

Step 1 - first place of disagreement.  
Let $\ell\ge 0$ be the smallest index with
$\mathbf b_\ell(\mathbf a)\neq\mathbf b_\ell(\mathbf c)$.
Pick the smallest
coordinate $k$ for which the bits differ; assume
$b_{\ell,k}(\mathbf a)=0,\;b_{\ell,k}(\mathbf c)=1$
(the other case is symmetric).

Step 2 - swapping those two bits only.  
Put  
 $\displaystyle\mathbf a'=\mathbf a+e_{k,\ell},\qquad
  \mathbf c'=\mathbf c-e_{k,\ell}\;.$
Because only one bit is toggled in each vector,  
(1) the sum is preserved:
$\mathbf a'+\mathbf c'=\mathbf a+\mathbf c=\mathbf n$;  
(2) each new vector is still non-negative (the subtracted bit was a
1); and  
(3) $\omega(\mathbf a')=\omega(\mathbf a)\!+\!1$,
$\omega(\mathbf c')=\omega(\mathbf c)\!+\!1$,
so $\mathbf a',\mathbf c'\in B$.

Step 3 - involution.  
Applying the same construction to $\{\mathbf a',\mathbf c'\}$ returns
$\{\mathbf a,\mathbf c\}$.  Hence  

 $\Phi:\{\mathbf a,\mathbf c\}\longmapsto\{\mathbf a',\mathbf c'\}$  

is a fixed-point-free involution between the two families  

 $\bigl\{\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}\subset A:
     \mathbf s_1\!+\!\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n\bigr\}$ and  
 $\bigl\{\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}\subset B:
     \mathbf s_1\!+\!\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n\bigr\}$.

Therefore $u_A(\mathbf n)=u_B(\mathbf n)$ for every
$\mathbf n$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.  Multivariate generating-function argument
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce
 $f(\mathbf x)=\sum_{\mathbf m\in A}\mathbf x^{\mathbf m},\;
  g(\mathbf x)=\sum_{\mathbf m\in B}\mathbf x^{\mathbf m}$,
$\mathbf x^{\mathbf m}=x_1^{m_1}\!\cdots x_d^{m_d}$.

B.1  Functional equations.  
Write $P(\mathbf x)=\prod_{k=1}^{d}(1+x_k)$ and
$Q(\mathbf x)=\prod_{k=1}^{d}(1-x_k)$.  Set  

 $H_{\text{even}}=\dfrac{P+Q}{2},\qquad
  H_{\text{odd}} =\dfrac{P-Q}{2}.$

Separating vectors according to the parities of their
least-significant bits gives  

 $f(\mathbf x)=H_{\text{even}}(\mathbf x)\,f(\mathbf x^{2})
                 +H_{\text{odd}} (\mathbf x)\,g(\mathbf x^{2}),$  
 $g(\mathbf x)=H_{\text{odd}} (\mathbf x)\,f(\mathbf x^{2})
                 +H_{\text{even}}(\mathbf x)\,g(\mathbf x^{2}).$ (1)

Because $A\sqcup B=\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$ we also have  

 $f(\mathbf x)+g(\mathbf x)=\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{d}\frac1{1-x_k}=Q^{-1}(\mathbf x).$ (2)

B.2  Eliminating $H_{\text{even}},H_{\text{odd}}$.  
Subtract the two identities in (1):

 $f-g = \bigl(H_{\text{even}}-H_{\text{odd}}\bigr)(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2}))
       = Q(\mathbf x)\,(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2})).$ (3)

Multiply (3) by (2):

 $(f-g)(f+g)=f^2-g^2=(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2})).$ (4)

B.3  Equality of pair-enumerators.  
Define  

 $P_A(\mathbf x)=\tfrac12\bigl(f(\mathbf x)^2-f(\mathbf x^{2})\bigr),\;
  P_B(\mathbf x)=\tfrac12\bigl(g(\mathbf x)^2-g(\mathbf x^{2})\bigr).$

Relation (4) rewrites as  
$f^2-g^2=f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2})$ and therefore  

 $P_A(\mathbf x)-P_B(\mathbf x)=\tfrac12\bigl[(f^2-g^2)-(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2}))\bigr]=0.$

Thus $P_A(\mathbf x)=P_B(\mathbf x)$, i.e. the coefficient of every
monomial $\mathbf x^{\mathbf n}$ is the same: $u_A(\mathbf n)=u_B(\mathbf n)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.  Uniqueness once the origin is fixed
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose a partition
$\mathbb N_0^{\,d}=A'\sqcup B'$ satisfies $u_{A'}(\mathbf n)=u_{B'}(\mathbf
n)$ for all $\mathbf n$ and $0\in A'$.  We prove by induction on the
graded lexicographic order that $A'=A$.

Induction step.  Assume all $\mathbf m\prec\mathbf n$ have been classified
and agree with $A$.  Examine the pair $\{0,\mathbf n\}$:
it contributes to $u_{A'}(\mathbf n)$ iff $\mathbf n\in A'$.
All other contributing pairs $\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}$ with
$\mathbf s_1+\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n$ involve smaller
summands, hence are already determined to balance.  Therefore the
equality $u_{A'}(\mathbf n)=u_{B'}(\mathbf n)$ forces the
contribution of $\{0,\mathbf n\}$ to be the same as in our original
colouring, whence $\mathbf n\in A$ iff $\mathbf n\in A'$.
Thus the colouring is unique once the colour of the origin is fixed.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Dimension jump:  the problem is now set in $\Bbb N_0^{\,d}$ with an
   arbitrary number $d\ge2$ of independent variables, so every argument
   must work simultaneously in all coordinates.

2. Two independent proofs are demanded:  
   • an explicit involution on *sets of lattice points*, which forces one to
     track bit patterns in several coordinates at once;  
   • a generating-function proof in $d$ variables, which requires fluency
     with multivariate formal power series, functional equations
     (2)–(3) and an iterative limiting argument.

3. Uniqueness is no longer a curiosity but a required theorem, and its
   proof has to handle a partially ordered $d$-dimensional lattice
   instead of the linear order $\Bbb N_0$.

4. None of the above can be solved by simple pattern-matching;
   one must recognise the higher-dimensional Thue–Morse structure and
   manage non-trivial algebraic manipulations in several variables.

These additions greatly deepen the theoretical content and the
technical workload compared with the original one-dimensional,
single-proof problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $d\ge 1$ and write every point  
$\mathbf x=(x_1,\dots ,x_d)\in\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$ in base 2,
     $x_k=\sum_{j\ge 0}2^j b_{j,k}$ with $b_{j,k}\in\{0,1\}$.
Put  

  $\displaystyle\omega(\mathbf x)=\sum_{j\ge 0}\sum_{k=1}^{d} b_{j,k}\pmod 2$  

(the total number of binary 1's in all coordinates, taken modulo 2) and define  

  $A=\{\mathbf x: \omega(\mathbf x)=0\},\qquad  
  B=\{\mathbf x: \omega(\mathbf x)=1\}. $  

For a set $S\subset\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$ let  

  $\displaystyle u_S(\mathbf n)=\#\bigl\{\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}\subset S:
        \mathbf s_1\ne\mathbf s_2,\;
        \mathbf s_1+\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n\bigr\}$  
(the sum is component-wise).

(a)  Prove that $u_A(\mathbf n)=u_B(\mathbf n)$ for every
    $\mathbf n\in\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$.  
    Supply two independent proofs:
        * a purely combinatorial fixed-point-free involution, and  
        * an argument with multivariate generating functions.

(b)  Show that, once the colour of the origin is prescribed, the
    partition $\mathbb N_0^{\,d}=A\sqcup B$ is unique up to swapping the
    two parts.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  For $\ell\ge 0$ set  
$e_{k,\ell}=2^{\ell}\mathbf e_k$ where $\mathbf e_k$ is the $k$-th unit
vector in $\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$; write $\mathbf b_\ell(\mathbf x)=(b_{\,\ell,1},\dots ,b_{\,\ell,d})$ for the $\ell$-th
binary column of $\mathbf x$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A.  Combinatorial bijection
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Input: an unordered pair
$\{\mathbf a,\mathbf c\}\subset A$ with $\mathbf a\!+\!\mathbf c=\mathbf n$
and $\mathbf a\neq\mathbf c$.

Step 1 - first place of disagreement.  
Let $\ell\ge 0$ be the smallest index with
$\mathbf b_\ell(\mathbf a)\neq\mathbf b_\ell(\mathbf c)$.
Pick the smallest
coordinate $k$ for which the bits differ; assume
$b_{\ell,k}(\mathbf a)=0,\;b_{\ell,k}(\mathbf c)=1$
(the other case is symmetric).

Step 2 - swapping those two bits only.  
Put  
 $\displaystyle\mathbf a'=\mathbf a+e_{k,\ell},\qquad
  \mathbf c'=\mathbf c-e_{k,\ell}\;.$
Because only one bit is toggled in each vector,  
(1) the sum is preserved:
$\mathbf a'+\mathbf c'=\mathbf a+\mathbf c=\mathbf n$;  
(2) each new vector is still non-negative (the subtracted bit was a
1); and  
(3) $\omega(\mathbf a')=\omega(\mathbf a)\!+\!1$,
$\omega(\mathbf c')=\omega(\mathbf c)\!+\!1$,
so $\mathbf a',\mathbf c'\in B$.

Step 3 - involution.  
Applying the same construction to $\{\mathbf a',\mathbf c'\}$ returns
$\{\mathbf a,\mathbf c\}$.  Hence  

 $\Phi:\{\mathbf a,\mathbf c\}\longmapsto\{\mathbf a',\mathbf c'\}$  

is a fixed-point-free involution between the two families  

 $\bigl\{\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}\subset A:
     \mathbf s_1\!+\!\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n\bigr\}$ and  
 $\bigl\{\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}\subset B:
     \mathbf s_1\!+\!\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n\bigr\}$.

Therefore $u_A(\mathbf n)=u_B(\mathbf n)$ for every
$\mathbf n$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.  Multivariate generating-function argument
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce
 $f(\mathbf x)=\sum_{\mathbf m\in A}\mathbf x^{\mathbf m},\;
  g(\mathbf x)=\sum_{\mathbf m\in B}\mathbf x^{\mathbf m}$,
$\mathbf x^{\mathbf m}=x_1^{m_1}\!\cdots x_d^{m_d}$.

B.1  Functional equations.  
Write $P(\mathbf x)=\prod_{k=1}^{d}(1+x_k)$ and
$Q(\mathbf x)=\prod_{k=1}^{d}(1-x_k)$.  Set  

 $H_{\text{even}}=\dfrac{P+Q}{2},\qquad
  H_{\text{odd}} =\dfrac{P-Q}{2}.$

Separating vectors according to the parities of their
least-significant bits gives  

 $f(\mathbf x)=H_{\text{even}}(\mathbf x)\,f(\mathbf x^{2})
                 +H_{\text{odd}} (\mathbf x)\,g(\mathbf x^{2}),$  
 $g(\mathbf x)=H_{\text{odd}} (\mathbf x)\,f(\mathbf x^{2})
                 +H_{\text{even}}(\mathbf x)\,g(\mathbf x^{2}).$ (1)

Because $A\sqcup B=\mathbb N_0^{\,d}$ we also have  

 $f(\mathbf x)+g(\mathbf x)=\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{d}\frac1{1-x_k}=Q^{-1}(\mathbf x).$ (2)

B.2  Eliminating $H_{\text{even}},H_{\text{odd}}$.  
Subtract the two identities in (1):

 $f-g = \bigl(H_{\text{even}}-H_{\text{odd}}\bigr)(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2}))
       = Q(\mathbf x)\,(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2})).$ (3)

Multiply (3) by (2):

 $(f-g)(f+g)=f^2-g^2=(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2})).$ (4)

B.3  Equality of pair-enumerators.  
Define  

 $P_A(\mathbf x)=\tfrac12\bigl(f(\mathbf x)^2-f(\mathbf x^{2})\bigr),\;
  P_B(\mathbf x)=\tfrac12\bigl(g(\mathbf x)^2-g(\mathbf x^{2})\bigr).$

Relation (4) rewrites as  
$f^2-g^2=f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2})$ and therefore  

 $P_A(\mathbf x)-P_B(\mathbf x)=\tfrac12\bigl[(f^2-g^2)-(f(\mathbf x^{2})-g(\mathbf x^{2}))\bigr]=0.$

Thus $P_A(\mathbf x)=P_B(\mathbf x)$, i.e. the coefficient of every
monomial $\mathbf x^{\mathbf n}$ is the same: $u_A(\mathbf n)=u_B(\mathbf n)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.  Uniqueness once the origin is fixed
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose a partition
$\mathbb N_0^{\,d}=A'\sqcup B'$ satisfies $u_{A'}(\mathbf n)=u_{B'}(\mathbf
n)$ for all $\mathbf n$ and $0\in A'$.  We prove by induction on the
graded lexicographic order that $A'=A$.

Induction step.  Assume all $\mathbf m\prec\mathbf n$ have been classified
and agree with $A$.  Examine the pair $\{0,\mathbf n\}$:
it contributes to $u_{A'}(\mathbf n)$ iff $\mathbf n\in A'$.
All other contributing pairs $\{\mathbf s_1,\mathbf s_2\}$ with
$\mathbf s_1+\mathbf s_2=\mathbf n$ involve smaller
summands, hence are already determined to balance.  Therefore the
equality $u_{A'}(\mathbf n)=u_{B'}(\mathbf n)$ forces the
contribution of $\{0,\mathbf n\}$ to be the same as in our original
colouring, whence $\mathbf n\in A$ iff $\mathbf n\in A'$.
Thus the colouring is unique once the colour of the origin is fixed.

1. Dimension jump:  the problem is now set in $\Bbb N_0^{\,d}$ with an
   arbitrary number $d\ge2$ of independent variables, so every argument
   must work simultaneously in all coordinates.

2. Two independent proofs are demanded:  
   • an explicit involution on *sets of lattice points*, which forces one to
     track bit patterns in several coordinates at once;  
   • a generating-function proof in $d$ variables, which requires fluency
     with multivariate formal power series, functional equations
     (2)–(3) and an iterative limiting argument.

3. Uniqueness is no longer a curiosity but a required theorem, and its
   proof has to handle a partially ordered $d$-dimensional lattice
   instead of the linear order $\Bbb N_0$.

4. None of the above can be solved by simple pattern-matching;
   one must recognise the higher-dimensional Thue–Morse structure and
   manage non-trivial algebraic manipulations in several variables.

These additions greatly deepen the theoretical content and the
technical workload compared with the original one-dimensional,
single-proof problem.
